request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"parse::devtools","name":"parse","one_liner":"MBaaS platform for mobile developers to store data, manage identity, handle push notifications, and run cloud code — acquired by Facebook in 2013 and shut down in January 2017.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both are B2B SaaS platforms charging for backend infrastructure access, but Parse used usage-metered pricing aimed at developers building apps, whereas the new pitch is a subscription-licensed assay-management platform for academic spinouts and small CROs. The underlying revenue mechanic (platform-as-a-service sold to technical builders) has surface overlap, but the domain, buyer, and pricing structure diverge substantially."},"market":{"score":1,"rationale":"Parse served a global mobile-developer ecosystem with hundreds of thousands of apps. The new pitch targets a narrow biotech niche — academic spinouts and small CROs — which is a completely different industry, regulatory environment, and buyer profile. The markets share almost no overlap."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Parse grew virally through developer adoption (bottom-up PLG), powered by YC network effects and eventually Facebook distribution. The new pitch targets institutional/lab buyers in biotech, implying a more direct sales or channel-partnership GTM. Both started with a small early-adopter community, but the motion differs materially."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Parse was an early-stage startup at founding (2011) before a major acquisition; the new pitch appears to be at a comparable pre-scale stage targeting a similarly niche early-adopter customer base. Both are infrastructure platforms with modest initial customer counts before attempting broader rollout."}},"why_similar":"Both are B2B infrastructure-as-a-service platforms that abstract away complex backend work for technical end-users, sold on a platform-license model. Parse offloaded mobile backend complexity (data storage, identity, push); the new pitch offloads wet-lab assay management complexity. Both target relatively small, technical early-adopter communities before scaling. Both face the classic platform risk of customers either building in-house or migrating to a competitor if the vendor disappears or pivots.","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges on every major dimension the Trusted facts describe for Parse: (1) Sector — biotech/life sciences vs. mobile developer tooling; (2) Customer type — academic spinouts and small CROs (institutional, regulated buyers) vs. individual developers; (3) Monetization — subscription/license vs. usage-metered; (4) Geography — the pitch implies a narrower, likely US/EU academic/CRO market vs. Parse's explicitly global developer community; (5) Regulatory exposure — lab assay management in biotech carries compliance obligations (GLP, 21 CFR Part 11) entirely absent from Parse's world.","failure_causes":["acquirer strategic deprioritization","single-customer dependency after Facebook acquisition","forced platform migration creating customer churn","no independent revenue sustainability post-acquisition","open-sourcing as exit rather than growth strategy","inability to compete with AWS/Google/Azure mobile backend offerings"],"lessons_for_input":["Never build your platform's survival around a single acquirer or strategic partner — design for independent commercial viability from day one.","Ensure customers can migrate away cleanly, but invest equally in retention mechanics so migration is never their default option.","Validate that your target buyers (academic spinouts, small CROs) have budget authority and procurement processes in place before scaling sales investment.","Build compliance features (GLP, 21 CFR Part 11) into the core product early — regulated biotech buyers will block adoption without them, and retrofitting is expensive.","Avoid usage-metered pricing traps for low-throughput scientific customers; a predictable subscription tier aligns better with lab budgeting cycles and reduces churn risk."]}
